title: Elaine And The Lame Duck

jokes.text: Take this cab to Cuba.
jokes.setup: Passenger gets in cab and gives destination
jokes.punchline: Destination is Cuba, like a hijacking
jokes.explanation: Sets up Walter as quirky but the joke is fairly obvious and weak

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: Cuba.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Cuba? That'll be quite a fare.

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: I don't care about the fare. Just get me to Cuba.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: This is a cab, not a plane. You want Cuba, you're gonna have to hijack this thing.

jokes.text: Did you hear that? I said Cuba, like... like a hijack.
jokes.setup: Walter's Cuba joke gets no reaction
jokes.punchline: He explains his own joke awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's social awkwardness by having him explain his own failed joke

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You see, it's funny because... Cuba is an island, and islands are surrounded by water, and when you think about it, water is wet, and wet things are... well, they're wet. So the joke is that if you go to Cuba, you might get wet. From the water. Which is why it's funny.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: No, wait, let me start over. The thing about Cuba is...

jokes.text: Don't you love life, driver? / No, we're just good friends.
jokes.setup: Romantic passenger asks if driver loves life
jokes.punchline: Driver treats 'love' literally - they're just friends
jokes.explanation: Classic wordplay with good economy and unexpected interpretation

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: Do you love life?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Love? No, life and I are just friends.

jokes.text: A-ha. You mean you want to go to... your place. / Desperately.
jokes.setup: Cab driver assumes woman wants romance at her place
jokes.punchline: She desperately wants to get away from her date
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup with efficient payoff

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So you want to come back to my place?

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: Oh thank God, yes! Please get me out of here!

jokes.text: He's right, you know. You can get killed in this neighborhood. / Well, in that case, let him out.
jokes.setup: Driver warns about dangerous neighborhood
jokes.punchline: Woman wants her awful date to get out there anyway
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with good setup/payoff structure

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You don't want to go down there. It's a very dangerous neighborhood.

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: That's okay. I'm on a date with a guy I can't stand. I'd be happy if he got mugged.

jokes.text: Did I say something to offend you tonight? / Everything after hello.
jokes.setup: Clueless date asks what he did wrong
jokes.punchline: Everything he said all night was offensive
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and escalation - covers the entire evening with one line

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: What did I do wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Everything you said all night was offensive.

jokes.text: My main course was ten pats of butter.
jokes.setup: She describes the awful restaurant experience
jokes.punchline: His French ordering resulted in just butter for her meal
jokes.explanation: Absurd visual of butter as main course is funny and specific

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: He ordered for both of us in French. I ended up with just butter.

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: Apparently he asked the waiter for 'butter' instead of whatever the actual dish was supposed to be.

jokes.text: What did you think that I meant when I said two hours ago, 'Please, for the love of God, take me home'? / I thought you were coming on to me.
jokes.setup: Woman describes her obvious distress signal
jokes.punchline: Walter completely misinterpreted desperation as seduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Walter's complete cluelessness with women

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: I was drowning! I was waving my arms and screaming for help!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I know. You looked so helpless... so vulnerable. I thought you were trying to tell me something.

dialogue.speaker: Jill
dialogue.line: I WAS trying to tell you something — that I was DROWNING!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Exactly. And the way you kept going under and coming back up... I thought it was some kind of signal.

jokes.text: I guess this means you don't want to sleep with me.
jokes.setup: After a disastrous evening and harsh rejection
jokes.punchline: Walter still asks about sex with no self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's obliviousness perfectly - still hopeful after total rejection

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You know, even after tonight, I still think I have a shot with her. Maybe next time I'll try a different approach. Say, do you think she'd be interested in... well, you know?

jokes.text: You have women in your cab all day long. I never meet anybody. / What do you do? / I'm a United States Congressman.
jokes.setup: Walter claims he never meets anybody
jokes.punchline: He's a Congressman - someone who should meet tons of people
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reveal - the one job where you should meet people constantly

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I never meet anybody.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you mean you never meet anybody? You're a Congressman!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Root canal by starlight.
jokes.setup: Louie took his mom to discount dentist who works nights
jokes.punchline: Alex makes it sound romantic with this phrase
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on romantic clichés applied to dental work

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, that's beautiful. Your mother and the dentist, alone together in the dark, the gentle whir of the drill...

jokes.text: Dad's a major contributor to the party, so, to thank him, they, uh, they ran me for Congress.
jokes.setup: Walter explains how he became a Congressman
jokes.punchline: He got the job as a favor to his dad, not on merit
jokes.explanation: Establishes Walter as unqualified and privileged

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I got the job as a favor to my dad, not on merit.

jokes.text: The week before the election my opponent was convicted of murder. / But you did win. / We had to have a runoff.
jokes.setup: Walter's opponent was convicted of murder
jokes.punchline: Walter still barely won and needed a runoff
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - losing to a convicted murderer is absurd enough, but needing a runoff makes it even funnier

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I still barely won and needed a runoff.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait, your opponent was convicted of murder and you still needed a runoff?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Yeah, apparently murder isn't as big a deal in this district as I thought.

jokes.text: There's talk of a recall.
jokes.setup: Alex tries to be encouraging about Walter being in office
jokes.punchline: Walter reveals even that's in jeopardy
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation of Walter's political incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Come on, Walter. You're a taxi driver. At least you have a job in the office.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, even that's in jeopardy.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm in your district. I voted for you. / Oh, yeah? Why? / The other guy was up for murder.
jokes.setup: Constituent proudly says he voted for Walter
jokes.punchline: Only reason was the opponent's murder charge
jokes.explanation: Callbacks the murder joke but doesn't add much new

dialogue.speaker: Constituent
dialogue.line: I voted for you.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Well, thank you very much!

dialogue.speaker: Constituent
dialogue.line: Yeah, the other guy had a murder charge.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: So you voted for me because my opponent had a murder charge?

dialogue.speaker: Constituent
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's the only reason.

jokes.text: Latka bowing to congressman and Alex stopping him
jokes.setup: Latka meets an important government official
jokes.punchline: He bows and tries to give money like in his home country
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka cultural misunderstanding bit

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You are very important man, yes? Here, please, take this money. Is custom in my country to show respect.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, no, you can't just give money to a government official. That's a bribe.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Bribe? No, no, is gift! In Caspian, when you meet official, you give money. Shows you are polite person.

jokes.text: She had to have a little work done but this guy's prices are so reasonable I even sprung for Novocaine. It's my own mother. What the hell?
jokes.setup: Louie talks about his mom's dental work
jokes.punchline: He usually wouldn't pay for pain relief but made an exception
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie characterization - cheap but showing minimal human decency

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: My mother just had all her teeth pulled. The dentist said, 'This is gonna hurt.' I said, 'Doc, you're talking about my mother — pain relief for her? That's not in my budget.' But then he told me what it would cost if she could still bite, so I said, 'Give her the gas.'

jokes.text: Said something about having to get up early and change her phone number.
jokes.setup: Walter explains why his date ended early
jokes.punchline: She needed to change her number to avoid him
jokes.explanation: Good example of Walter's oblivious self-reporting

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: She needed to change her number to avoid him.

jokes.text: In my country, women work like ox. They cook, they... have lots of babies, uh... they get old early. They happy.
jokes.setup: Latka describes women in his country
jokes.punchline: Despite hard lives, they're happy
jokes.explanation: Typical Latka cultural commentary, somewhat dated

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: In my country, the women, they work in the fields all day. They carry water from the well. They cook and they clean and they raise many children. Their hands, they are rough like the bark of trees.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But you know what? They are happy. They sing while they work. They laugh with their families. They don't need much to be content.

jokes.text: In America, the women, they have, uh... they have maids, they dress with nice clothes, they take bubble bath. They happy. / What is your point? / Women happy people.
jokes.setup: Latka gives long comparison of women across cultures
jokes.punchline: His profound conclusion is just that women are happy
jokes.explanation: Anticlimactic punchline after buildup, classic Latka style

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, I've traveled to many countries, studied many cultures. In France, the women are sophisticated and elegant. In Italy, they are passionate and romantic. In Japan, they are graceful and serene. In Sweden, they are independent and strong. But after all my research, all my observations across the world, I have come to one profound conclusion...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Women are happy.

jokes.text: You know how embarrassing it is to go to the presidential inaugural ball stag? I only hope people think I'm gay.
jokes.setup: Walter describes his dating problems
jokes.punchline: He'd rather be thought gay than pathetic
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's desperation and unique social position

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: He'd rather be thought gay than pathetic

jokes.text: A woman once said to me, 'I never want to see you again as long as I live.' / That's happened to everybody. / We hadn't been introduced.
jokes.setup: Tony shares a harsh rejection story to comfort Walter
jokes.punchline: The rejection happened before they even met
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke with perfect timing and economy

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think that's bad? I once asked a woman out, and you know what she said? She said, 'I'd rather wait for a man who hasn't been divorced three times.' So I said, 'Lady, I haven't been divorced three times.' She said, 'I know. I'm waiting.'

jokes.text: Damn it, Louie, you little twerp / She's much nicer than what I'm used to.
jokes.setup: Elaine angrily berates Louie
jokes.punchline: Walter thinks this outburst makes her nicer than his usual dates
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing how low Walter's standards are

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Louie, you are the most disgusting, vile, reprehensible excuse for a human being I have ever had the misfortune of knowing!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You know, she's nicer than the women I usually date.

jokes.text: You thanked me. You thanked my children, you thanked my baby-sitter...
jokes.setup: Walter says he thanked her
jokes.punchline: He thanked everyone remotely connected to the evening
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's over-politeness and nervousness

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He thanked everyone remotely connected to the evening

jokes.text: I guess you can say 'thank you' too often.
jokes.setup: Elaine points out his excessive thanking
jokes.punchline: Walter learns this lesson in real time
jokes.explanation: Mild character beat showing Walter's social learning

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You thank people too much, Walter.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Thank you for pointing that out.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: See? You just did it again.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You're right. I apologize.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And thank you is not an apology.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Thank you for clarifying that.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: There you go again!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I can't help it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just stop saying thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Thank you for your patience.

jokes.text: I perspire freely. I'm talking buckets. That's why I'll never be reelected. Who's going to vote for a man who always looks like he just stepped out of a sauna?
jokes.setup: Walter admits he sweats
jokes.punchline: He thinks it will doom his political career
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that connects personal and professional insecurities

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I sweat. I sweat a lot. But I'm not worried about it.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I am worried. If people find out I sweat, it'll ruin my political career.

jokes.text: I shake hands with people, and they wipe their hands off. They pretend to do something else so they won't hurt my feelings. They, uh... they cross their arms. They adjust their ties. They wave hello at friends.
jokes.setup: People wipe off their hands after shaking with him
jokes.punchline: List of increasingly elaborate cover-ups people use
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list with visual comedy potential

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: People wipe off their hands after shaking with me. I've seen all the cover-ups. First they try to be subtle—just a quick wipe on their pants. Then they escalate to the bathroom, washing up real good. One guy went home and took a shower. Another fellow jumped in the river.

jokes.text: Could we talk about something else? I'm talking about sweat again. Why do I always talk sweat on dates? It's because I'm always sweating on dates.
jokes.setup: Walter realizes he's obsessing over sweating
jokes.punchline: He's trapped in a vicious cycle of sweat-talk and actual sweat
jokes.explanation: Self-aware character moment that shows Walter's neuroses perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm sweating because I'm worried about sweating, which makes me sweat more, which makes me worry more about sweating.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: It's a vicious cycle!

jokes.text: He hates me. / Who? / The waiter. I know when a man despises me and that man despises me. He's going to spit in my food.
jokes.setup: Walter thinks waiter hates him
jokes.punchline: He's convinced of impending food sabotage
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's paranoia and self-consciousness

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: He's got it out for me, I know it. He's going to spit in my soup.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Walter, why would he spit in your soup?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Because I sent back the eggs! One time, fifteen years ago, I sent back the eggs, and he's never forgiven me.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Is it? Then why does he keep staring at me? Why does he keep mumbling under his breath? He's planning something, I can feel it.

jokes.text: Could we go back to sweating?
jokes.setup: Walter's paranoia about the waiter
jokes.punchline: Elaine prefers his sweat obsession to this new neurosis
jokes.explanation: Great callback with perfect economy - choosing the lesser of two evils

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: At least when you were obsessed with your sweat, you were functional. This paranoia about the waiter is worse.

jokes.text: What, they run out of towels in the men's room? / No, that's, uh, that's sweat.
jokes.setup: Louie sees Walter's wet appearance
jokes.punchline: Walter admits it's just sweat, not water
jokes.explanation: Visual callback to Walter's sweating problem

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What happened to you? You're soaking wet!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: It's not water, Louie. It's just sweat.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sweat? From what?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I've been nervous all day.

jokes.text: Talk about a lame duck.
jokes.setup: Discussing Walter the Congressman
jokes.punchline: Louie uses political term 'lame duck' literally about Walter
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay connecting political term to Walter's pathetic nature

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Walter's a lame duck congressman.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I saw him trying to walk up the steps at City Hall. It took him twenty minutes.

jokes.text: Well, you see, there's schlemiels and shlimazls. A schlemiel is a guy who goes to a formal dinner party and spills his soup. A shlimazl's the guy he spills it on.
jokes.setup: Explaining difference between schlemiels and shlimazls
jokes.punchline: Clear definition with concrete example
jokes.explanation: Classic definition joke with clear setup/payoff and cultural authenticity from Louie

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: A schlemiel is a guy who spills soup on a shlimazel. A shlimazel is the guy who the soup gets spilled on.

jokes.text: I was hoping you had an answer.
jokes.setup: Louie asks what's good about him
jokes.punchline: Alex admits he has no idea either
jokes.explanation: Good deflating comeback about Louie's character

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's good about me?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I have no idea either.

jokes.text: Walter, your menu's on fire.
jokes.setup: Normal restaurant scene
jokes.punchline: Walter has somehow set his menu on fire
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing Walter's tendency to create disasters

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Walter, your menu is on fire.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I know.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Aren't you going to do something about it?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm reading the specials.

jokes.text: I think I'll start with the chocolate cheesecake.
jokes.setup: After Walter sets menu on fire
jokes.punchline: Elaine calmly orders dessert first, giving up on normal dinner
jokes.explanation: Good reaction showing Elaine's resignation to chaos

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'll just have dessert.

jokes.text: Boy, this guy. He is a horse's yatabe.
jokes.setup: Louie commenting on Walter's disasters
jokes.punchline: Uses made-up Yiddish-sounding curse word
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Louie creative profanity

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Walter, you're a complete meshuggeneh! Every time you open your mouth, something falls apart. You know what you are? You're a total schlemiel-schmendrick-schmaltzoon!

jokes.text: I never want to see me again.
jokes.setup: Walter talking about women not wanting to see him
jokes.punchline: He doesn't even want to see himself
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating line showing Walter's rock-bottom self-esteem

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: He doesn't even want to see himself

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? You want to go out Saturday night? / Oh, no, Saturday, I made these... / See? See? What about Sunday? / No, I take the kids... / Mm-hmm, mm-hmm. What about Monday? / Monday's a bad day. / Mm-hmm. Tuesday?
jokes.setup: Walter rapid-fire asking for dates
jokes.punchline: He goes through entire week getting rejected
jokes.explanation: Good escalating sequence showing Walter's desperation

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: How about Monday? No? Tuesday? Wednesday? Thursday? Friday? Saturday? Sunday?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no, no, no, and no.

jokes.text: What's wrong with you?
jokes.setup: Elaine agrees to see him again
jokes.punchline: Walter questions her sanity for wanting to date him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Walter's self-awareness about his undesirability

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You want to go out with me again?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Are you feeling all right?

jokes.text: A shlimazl is a guy who comes home from work early and finds his wife in bed with his boss, then gets docked a day's pay for leaving the office.
jokes.setup: Callback to earlier shlimazl definition
jokes.punchline: More elaborate example of ultimate bad luck
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the shlimazl concept with darkly comic specificity

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You wanna know what a real shlimazl is? I'll tell you what a real shlimazl is.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A shlimazl is a guy who goes to a fancy restaurant, orders the soup of the day, and the waiter says, 'Sorry, that'll be five extra dollars.' So he says, 'Why?' The waiter says, 'Because today we're serving it in a bowl.'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Or a guy who wins the lottery. Fifty million bucks. On the way to claim his prize, he gets hit by a truck. He survives, goes to the lottery office in a wheelchair, and they tell him, 'Congratulations! Your check is ready.' He says, 'Great!' They hand it to him—it blows out the window.

jokes.text: Listen, Nardo... if you ever grab me again in a non-erogenous zone, you're gone.
jokes.setup: Elaine grabs Louie's shirt
jokes.punchline: Louie's weirdly specific threat about touching zones
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Louie creepiness with specific phrasing

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen, you touch me again and I'm gonna report you for sexual harassment. And I'm very specific about my zones.

jokes.text: I'm drawn to that scrawny little puppy in the pet store, you know, that nobody else wants.
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining why she likes Walter
jokes.punchline: Compares him to unwanted pet store puppy
jokes.explanation: Good analogy that captures both affection and pity

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's like that puppy in the pet store window that nobody wants. Everybody walks by, they look at him, they go 'Ugh, not that one.' But I see something special in him.

jokes.text: So I look over at Walter, and he's got tears in his eyes. So then I got them both on my hands.
jokes.setup: Walter empathizes with crying child
jokes.punchline: Now Elaine has two crying people to deal with
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Walter's emotional sensitivity backfiring

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Now I have two crying people to deal with.

jokes.text: Like, 'How about a roll in the hay?' Walter's suggestions seem to run toward the blunt, don't they?
jokes.setup: Walter makes sexual suggestions
jokes.punchline: Understatement about his lack of subtlety
jokes.explanation: Understated observation about Walter's crude approach

jokes.text: You want advice? You want to break up with Walter, fine. Just go on your own instincts, those same instincts that have gotten you this far... Divorced, poor, working nights, holding two jobs.
jokes.setup: Alex sarcastically suggests trusting her instincts
jokes.punchline: Lists all the ways her instincts have failed her
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list with biting sarcasm about Elaine's life choices

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, trust my instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My instincts told me to marry Dennis. My instincts told me to invest in that restaurant. My instincts told me to take that job in Pittsburgh.

jokes.text: Oh. You're going to go to bed with him.
jokes.setup: Elaine says she'll do what she has to do
jokes.punchline: Alex realizes she's choosing sex over breakup
jokes.explanation: Good realization beat with understated delivery

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'll do what I have to do.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait, you're not going to break up with him?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're choosing sex over a breakup?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, when you put it that way...

jokes.text: Maybe just a little... applause.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks if he wants anything
jokes.punchline: Walter wants applause for his sexual performance
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awkward Walter moment showing his need for validation

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Do you want anything?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Yeah, applause for my sexual performance.

jokes.text: I've never sweat so much in my entire life.
jokes.setup: Morning after intimate encounter
jokes.punchline: Walter focuses on sweating instead of romance
jokes.explanation: Callback to sweating obsession at inappropriate romantic moment

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about last night.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: All that sweating... I'm gonna need a larger shirt.

jokes.text: I had a little time to think before you woke up this morning... seven, eight hours...
jokes.setup: Walter says he had time to think
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was awake all night overthinking
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter's neurotic tendency to overthink everything

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I had time to think.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I was awake all night overthinking.

jokes.text: Walter, you want to fly your parents to New York because of what we did last night? / It's a shuttle.
jokes.setup: Walter wants to fly parents to meet Elaine after one night
jokes.punchline: He minimizes the gesture because it's just a shuttle flight
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Walter's inability to understand appropriate relationship pacing

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm flying your parents in from New Jersey. It's just a shuttle flight.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just a shuttle flight? Walter, that's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: It's no big deal. I do it all the time.

jokes.text: How about every Thursday night for the next three months? Is 8:00 okay?
jokes.setup: Walter agrees they should take it slow
jokes.punchline: Immediately schedules a rigid relationship calendar
jokes.explanation: Perfect Walter moment - agreeing to take it slow while immediately structuring everything

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: You're right, we should take it slow.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I've already blocked out Tuesday and Thursday evenings, alternating weekends, and I'm thinking dinner the first Saturday of every month.

jokes.text: It was, I guess, an ordinary evening for most people.
jokes.setup: Alex asks if anything out of the ordinary happened
jokes.punchline: Walter implies sex is ordinary for most people, just not him
jokes.explanation: Subtle way of revealing his inexperience while maintaining dignity

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Did anything out of the ordinary happen?

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Out of the ordinary? No, nothing. Sex is pretty ordinary for most people.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: Just not for me.

jokes.text: there are some things a gentleman does not discuss.
jokes.setup: Walter claims to be discreet
jokes.punchline: Ironic since he's been obviously hinting about it
jokes.explanation: Shows Walter trying to be dignified after being obvious about his night

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: I'm the soul of discretion. I never tell anyone's secrets.

dialogue.speaker: Other character
dialogue.line: Really? Because you've been dropping hints all week about what happened.

dialogue.speaker: Walter
dialogue.line: What hints? I haven't said a word!

dialogue.speaker: Other character
dialogue.line: You literally told everyone at the garage yesterday.

tmdb_overview: Elaine dates a congressman who badly needs self-confidence.
insight: Mid-tier Taxi leans hard on character comedy but struggles to land memorable punchlines.
